div.div: Effect: Three knaves (jacks) are hidden in different parts of the deck; the King (constable) finds them all in one place. Method: hold the four jacks and the King of Spades. As you narrate the story — 'The first knave hides in the attic, the second in the cellar, the third behind the kitchen' — appear to slide the jacks to different positions, but secretly stack them together with indifferent cards hidden between them. Place the King on top. One dramatic cut of the deck brings the King face-to-face with all three jacks. The story is the misdirection.

Narrative card tricks — in which a story both explains the sequence of moves and completely hides the method — are considered among the most elegant forms of card magic. The audience is too busy following the story to notice what your hands are doing.

A story is told: three knaves (thieves) go to different parts of a house while a constable searches. The King (constable) eventually finds all three together. The trick: the three knaves are placed at specific positions in a single pile, and a cut brings the King to join them.

svg.text: Source: The Magician's Own Book (1862) · Heritage Parlor
